query: What is the Eightfold Path in Buddhism?
response: The Eightfold Path in Buddhism is a central teaching that guides practitioners towards the cessation of suffering. It is described as the ["way of practice leading to the cessation of stress"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha), *Samyutta Nikaya 56.11 - Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta*. This path includes eight components: right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. The Buddha emphasized that the Eightfold Path is found in any true Dhamma and Discipline, marking the presence of a ["true ascetic of the first, second, third, and fourth degrees of saintliness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha), *Digha Nikaya 16 - Mahaparinibbana Sutta*.

query: How do different religions view the afterlife?
response: In exploring the afterlife, various religious traditions offer distinct perspectives. In Islam, the Qur'án states that ["every soul shall taste of death"](https://oceanlibrary.com/quran_rodwell-tr?paraId=para_292) and emphasizes the promise of Paradise for those who believe and do good deeds. Christianity echoes this with Jesus' assurance that ["he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john?paraId=para_425), highlighting eternal life through faith. In Sikhism, the Guru Granth Sahib speaks of the soul's journey, noting that it "wanders through countless incarnations" until it finds peace. Buddhism, on the other hand, views Nirvāṇa as ["the highest happiness"](https://oceanlibrary.com/dhammapada_buddha?paraId=para_230), a state beyond the cycle of rebirth. Hinduism, through the Bhagavad Gita, teaches that the spirit is ["birthless and deathless"](https://oceanlibrary.com/bhagavad-gita?paraId=para_30), emphasizing the eternal nature of the soul. In the Bahá'í Faith, the afterlife is described as the soul's progression to a "world of Eternal Existence". Taoism sees life and death as part of a continuous cycle, where "existence and non-existence" are harmonized. Lastly, Judaism holds that the spirit returns to God, as expressed in Ecclesiastes: ["the spirit returns to God who gave it"](https://oceanlibrary.com/zohar-volume-1?paraId=para_1161). Each tradition offers a unique lens through which to view the journey beyond this life.

query: How do Buddhism and Hinduism differ on the concept of self?
response: In Hinduism, the concept of self is deeply intertwined with the idea of the [Atman](https://oceanlibrary.com/hymns-of-atharva-veda), which is considered the true self or soul that is distinct from the body and mind, as suggested in the Upanishads. This Atman is seen as eternal and is often described as being united with the body, senses, and mind, making it the ["enjoyer"](https://oceanlibrary.com/hymns-of-atharva-veda) of experiences. In contrast, Buddhism challenges the notion of a permanent self, emphasizing the concept of [anatta](https://oceanlibrary.com/hymns-of-atharva-veda), or "non-self," which suggests that what we consider the self is merely a collection of changing phenomena. This fundamental difference highlights the Hindu focus on a permanent, unchanging soul versus the Buddhist view of impermanence and the absence of a fixed self.

query: Compare the creation stories across religions in the library
response:
Creation stories across various religious traditions offer unique perspectives on the origins of the universe. In Christianity, the Gospel of John begins with the profound assertion that ["in the beginning was the Word"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-gospel-of-john/?paraId=para_8), emphasizing the divine logos as the source of all creation. In Judaism, the Book of Genesis famously recounts God's command, ["Let there be light: and there was light"](https://oceanlibrary.com/kjv-first-book-of-moses/?paraId=para_10), marking the start of the created world.
